herb_constituents.name: saponins

herb_constituents.name: luteolin

herb_constituents.name: sterols

herb_constituents.name: caffeic acid

herb_constituents.name: kaempferol

herb_constituents.name: apigenin

herb_constituents.name: phytosterols

plant_constituents.name: allicin
plant_constituents.desc: Allium sativum allicin, a sulfur-containing compound, is a key bioactive constituent responsible for the medicinal properties of garlic. It has been reported to exhibit antibacterial, antiviral, and antifungal activities, as well as anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. The production of allicin from alliin, a non-enzymatic reaction, is crucial for its medicinal properties to be activated.

plant_constituents.name: diallyl disulfide
plant_constituents.desc: Allium sativum diallyl disulfide is a bioactive compound found in garlic, responsible for its medicinal and therapeutic properties. It has been shown to exhibit antimicrobial, antiviral, and antifungal activities, making it effective in preventing infections and promoting wound healing. Additionally, diallyl disulfide has been found to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, contributing to its potential in treating various health conditions.

plant_constituents.name: diallyl trisulfide
plant_constituents.desc: Allium sativum diallyl trisulfide, a key bioactive compound found in garlic, has been studied for its potential health benefits, including its ability to exhibit antimicrobial, antiviral, and antioxidant properties. Research suggests that diallyl trisulfide may help to reduce inflammation and improve cardiovascular health by inhibiting platelet aggregation and lowering triglycerides. Additionally, this compound has been shown to have anti-cancer properties, with studies indicating its potential to inhibit the growth of cancer cells and induce apoptosis.

plant_constituents.name: s-allylcysteine sulfoxide
plant_constituents.desc: Allium sativum s-allylcysteine sulfoxide is a key bioactive compound found in garlic, contributing to its medicinal properties. This compound is a precursor to allicin, a well-known bioactive molecule responsible for garlic's pungent smell and numerous health benefits. Research has shown that s-allylcysteine sulfoxide may have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, potentially aiding in the prevention and treatment of various diseases.
